metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_1614
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Pang et al generate syt-2 mutant mice, in which the 5' end of syt-2 gene is replaced by lacZ. The authors use the beta-galactosidase as a reporter unter syt-2 promoter in order to find out the brain regions with high syt-2 expression (see Fig. 1 and Table 1). Electrophysiological studies in striatal neurons reveal slow kinetics of evoked release without changes in the total amount of release. syt-2-deficient NMJ exhibit a large reduction of evoked release and increased frequency of spontaneous miniature releases. Taken together, the data shows that syt-2, in orchestration with other synaptotagmins,mediates Ca2+-mediated neurotransmitter release.
In detail, prior to electrophysiological recordings Pang et al report on the brain region expression of syt-2 based on the reporter gene detection (majority of neurons in striatum and zona incerta, reticular nucleus of thalamus, ventromedial nucleus of hypothalamus, brainstem, spinal cord, cerebellum, NMJs; fewer neurons in hippocampus and cortex - suggested to be inhibitory based on their location). The electrophysiological recordings (IPSCs induced by focal stimulation) are performed in cultured neurons isolated from dorsal neostriatum and show slow time course of synaptic charge transfer in mut neurons (Fig. 6D), with mean decay times for fast and slow constituents of charge transfer being significantly increased upon syt-2 deletion (Fig. 6 E,F). Additionally, Pang et al demonstrate that the change in the delayed release are due to presynaptic defect caused by the syt-2 deletion (Fig. 6 H-K).
Significant (Ca2+-independent) increase in miniature endplate potentials (mEPPs), indicative for spontaneous neurotransmitter release at the NMJ of the mut animals, is recorded (Fig. 7). In addition, the amplitude and quantal content of the EPP at syt-2 KO NMJs are decreased two fold (Fig.8 B, C) indicating less acetylcholine release at the NMJs of mut mice. Simultaneously, the speed of EPP increase is reduced in KO mice, which, with respect to unchanged amplitudes and rise kinetics of mEPPs, indicate impairment of Ca2+-triggered SV release. In line with the described findings, the authors demonstrate that changes in evoked EPPs are caused by reduced initial release probability (causing PPF without depression - Fig. 9 and 10). Moreover, they monitor desynchronization of evoked response at NMJ in syt-2-deficient mice upon high frequency stimulus trains indicating impairment of synchronous release due to syt-2 loss (fig. 11).. Experimental description: Materials and Methods
Generation and maintenance of synaptotagmin-2 knock-out mice. The targeting
vector (see Fig. 1A) was constructed using routine techniques
